subUnits.title: 6.1 Longitudinal, Transverse Waves & Wave Properties
notes: Types and Properties of Waves
Key Concepts:
1. Transverse Waves:
- Particle vibration perpendicular to wave direction
- Examples: light waves, water surface waves, rope waves
- Can be polarized
2. Longitudinal Waves:
- Particle vibration parallel to wave direction
- Examples: sound waves, compression waves in springs
- Consist of compressions and rarefactions
3. Wave Equation:
- Y = A sin(ωt - 2πx/λ)
- Y = displacement, A = amplitude, ω = angular frequency, t = time, x = position, λ = wavelength
4. Wave Properties:
a) Reflection: Bouncing back at a boundary
b) Refraction: Bending due to speed change in different media
c) Diffraction: Spreading around obstacles or through gaps
d) Interference: Superposition of waves (constructive/destructive)
e) Polarization: Restricting vibration to one plane (transverse only)
5. Nigerian Context:
- Sound waves (longitudinal) in traditional drums and local music
- Water wave diffraction at Nigerian river mouths
- Interference patterns in ripple tank practicals
Conclusion:
- Transverse: vibration ⊥ direction; Longitudinal: vibration ∥ direction
- Wave equation links displacement, amplitude, frequency, and position
- Properties: reflection, refraction, diffraction, interference, polarization
- Mastery supports acoustics, optics, and engineering applications

quiz.q: In transverse waves, particle vibration is:
quiz.explanation: Transverse waves have particle vibration perpendicular to the direction of wave propagation.
Sound waves are longitudinal waves with compressions and rarefactions.

quiz.q: The wave equation Y = A sin(ωt - 2πx/λ) describes:
opts: Wave displacement over time and position
quiz.explanation: The wave equation describes displacement (Y) as a function of time (t) and position (x).
Diffraction is the spreading of waves when passing through gaps or around obstacles.
Polarization restricts vibration to one plane and only occurs in transverse waves.

quiz.q: In Nigerian context, longitudinal waves are demonstrated using:
quiz.explanation: Drum beats and sound are common examples of longitudinal waves in Nigerian classrooms.

opts: Crest meets crest or trough meets trough
quiz.explanation: Constructive interference occurs when waves are in phase (crest meets crest).

quiz.q: A common misconception about waves is:
quiz.explanation: Only transverse waves can be polarized; longitudinal waves cannot.

quiz.q: When a wave enters a denser medium, it:
opts: Slows down and bends toward normal
quiz.explanation: Waves slow down and bend toward the normal when entering a denser medium (refraction).

quiz.q: To demonstrate interference in school, you should use:
opts: A ripple tank with two sources
quiz.explanation: A ripple tank with two coherent sources clearly demonstrates wave interference patterns.
